content.full_text:
Ravi Mehta
Chairman

Fair Political Practices Commission

July 17, 1996


Frank Wallace
Consultant
Education Technology Office
California Department of Education
721 Capitol Mall
Sacramento, CA 95814

Re: Your Request for Assistance
Our File No. A-96-196

Dear Mr. Wallace:

This is in response to your letter requesting advice regarding your responsibilities as an employee of the California Department of Education under the "revolving-door" provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1

QUESTIONS

1. As a public official with the California Department of Education, what restrictions are imposed on your participation in the department's matters relating to persons with whom you may be negotiating prospective employment?

2. At the time you terminate or take a leave of absence from your employment with the California Department of Education, what restrictions will there be on your post-employment activity?

CONCLUSIONS

1. As a designated employee of the California Department of Education, you are prohibited from making, participating in making, or using your official position to influence, any governmental decision directly relating to any person with whom you are negotiating prospective employment.

1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. All references to regulations are to Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations.

428 J Street, Suite 800, Sacramento, California 95814 (916) 322-5660

OUR FILE NO. A-96-196
Page 2


     2.  You may not represent or act as agent or attorney for any
person before the California Department of Education for compensation, for the purpose of influencing administrative or
legislative action for one year.

     In addition, you may not represent any other person before
the California Department of Education with respect to any
program in which you participated or monitored. This restriction
has no time limit.

                              FACTS

     You currently work in the Education Technology Office of the
California Department of Education. You are considering an
employment opportunity as the Director of the California Computer
Refurbishing Fund, which will be administered by the Sacramento
County Office of Education. The legal authorization for the
California Computer Refurbishing Fund was the chaptering of
AB 835. According to your letter and the various attachments, you
were not involved in the development of the guidelines for the
program; you were not involved in reviewing the applications for
the administration of the program; you were not involved and had
no input in the selection of the Sacramento County Office of
Education to administer the program; and you will be resigning
from your position with the California Department of Education or
taking a leave of absence if you are offered and accept the
position with the County of Sacramento.

     Pursuant to our conversation on June 28, 1996, you indicated
to me that you are a designated employee with the California
Department of Education and have full disclosure.

                             ANALYSIS

Influencing Prospective Employment

     As a designated employee with the California Department of
Education, you are prohibited from making, participating in
making, or using your official position to influence, any
governmental decision directly relating to any person with whom
you are negotiating, or have any arrangement concerning,
prospective employment. (Section 87407.)

     The conflict-of-interest definitions of making, participating
in making or influencing a decision may be used to interpret these
terms in Section 87407. (Chalmers Advice Letter, No. I-91-366;
See Regulations 18700-18700.1.) Pursuant to these definitions, a
public official is using his or her official position to influence
a decision where the official contacts, or appeals before, or
otherwise attempts to influence, any member, officer, employee or
consultant of the official's agency or any agency appointed by or
subject to the budgetary control of his or her agency.
(Regulation 18700.1.) Moreover, an official is using his or her
official position to influence a decision before any other agency.

Our File No. A-96-196
Page 3


if, for the purpose of influencing the decision, the official acts
or purports to act on behalf of, or as the representative of, his
or her agency.

According to your facts, your question is premised on the
possibility of accepting a position as the Director of the
California Computer Refurbishing Funds for the Sacramento County
Office of Education. You have submitted an application for the
position. Thus, you are currently "negotiating" or making an
"arrangement" concerning prospective employment.

Governmental decisions "directly relate" to a potential
employer, if the decision will financially affect the potential
employer, or if the employer has attempted to influence the agency
in any manner with respect to the decision. (Chalmers Advice
Letter, supra.) Based on your letter and the attached materials,
you did not make any governmental decisions which were directly
related to your potential employer, you did not have any influence
as to the selection of the grant award recipient, nor did you
determine the nature of the program proposal to implement this new
program. Therefore, based on these facts, Section 87407 would not
apply.

Post-Employment Restrictions

The one year "revolving door" restrictions in the Act would
prohibit you from representing or acting as agent or attorney for
any person before the California Department of Education for
compensation, for the purpose of influencing administrative or
legislative action for one year. In addition, you may not
represent any other person before the California Department of
Education with respect to any program in which you participated or
monitored. This "switching sides" restriction has no time limit.

Attached is an outline which provides a detailed discussion
regarding both prohibitions. If you need specific advice on
revolving door issues should you be offered and accept the
position with the County of Sacramento, please contact our office.

If you are offered the position as Director of the California
Refurbishing Fund with the County of Sacramento and decide to take
a leave of absence from your position with the Department of
Education rather than resigning, the revolving door restrictions
would still apply. In addition, we suggest that you contact the
Attorney General's office regarding other provisions of law which
may apply, such as the doctrine of incompatible offices.

Our File No. A-96-196
Page 4


          I hope this outline is helpful to you.  If I can be of any
further assistance, please feel free to contact me.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        Steven G. Churchwell
                                        General Counsel

                                        [signature]
                                By:     Lynda Doherty
                                        Political Reform Consultant
                                        Legal Division

Enclosure
